era_context: Late Bronze Age, during the reigns of Ramesses II (Egypt, r. 1279–1213 BCE) and Muwatalli II (Hittites, r. c. 1295–1272 BCE). This was a period of great-power competition and international trade in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The Mycenaean Greek world was in its Late Helladic IIIB phase, at the height of palatial culture. In China, the Shang Dynasty was in its final centuries. In Mesoamerica, the Preclassic period was ongoing, with no written records.

events.title: Battle of Kadesh (traditional date; actual date disputed)
events.description: The Battle of Kadesh is traditionally dated to 1274 BCE, but some scholars place it as early as 1300 BCE or as late as 1268 BCE. The battle was fought between the Egyptian forces of Ramesses II and the Hittite forces of Muwatalli II over control of the Levant, particularly the city of Kadesh and the region of Amurru. The battle was one of the largest chariot engagements in ancient history, involving thousands of troops. The outcome was inconclusive, with both sides claiming victory. The battle led to a peace treaty between Egypt and the Hittites in 1259 BCE (or thereabouts), the earliest known international peace treaty. The battle is documented in Egyptian temple reliefs and inscriptions, particularly at the Ramesseum and Abu Simbel.
key_figures: Amun-her-khepeshef (Egyptian general, son of Ramesses II)

sources.name: Egyptian temple reliefs at the Ramesseum and Abu Simbel
Hittite royal inscriptions and correspondence (Boğazköy archives)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Kadesh is one of the best-documented events of the Late Bronze Age, but its precise date remains debated. Traditional Egyptian chronology places it in 1274 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates ranging from 1300 to 1268 BCE. The discrepancy arises from uncertainties in correlating Egyptian king lists with astronomical observations and other chronological markers. For the purposes of this research, the traditional date of 1274 BCE is used, but the event is listed here as a placeholder for the broader period of Egyptian-Hittite conflict.

events.title: Mycenaean trade networks and cultural exchange
events.description: The Mycenaean Greek world maintained extensive trade networks throughout the Mediterranean and into the Near East during this period. Mycenaean pottery, bronze goods, and other products were traded to Egypt, the Levant, Anatolia, and beyond. In return, Mycenaean elites imported luxury goods such as ivory, lapis lazuli, and cedar. This trade was facilitated by a maritime merchant class and supported by the palatial centers. The trade networks would be disrupted during the Late Bronze Age Collapse around 1200 BCE.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of Mycenaean pottery and goods found throughout the Mediterranean
Linear B tablets recording trade transactions

events.certainty_note: The existence of Mycenaean trade networks is well-established through archaeological evidence.

events.title: Shang Dynasty bronze-working and oracle bone script (China)
events.description: The Shang Dynasty of China was renowned for its advanced bronze-working technology and the use of oracle bone script for divination and record-keeping. Bronze vessels, weapons, and ritual objects were produced in large quantities and served both practical and ceremonial purposes. Oracle bones (ox scapulae and turtle plastrons) were inscribed with questions posed to ancestors and deities, and the answers were interpreted through heat-induced cracks. This practice provides valuable insights into Shang society, religion, and governance.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of bronze vessels and weapons
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The existence of Shang bronze-working and oracle bone script is confirmed by archaeological and epigraphic evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Kadesh is one of the most contentious issues in ancient Near Eastern chronology. While the traditional date of 1274 BCE is widely accepted, some scholars argue for dates as early as 1300 BCE or as late as 1268 BCE. This discrepancy has significant implications for the entire chronology of the Late Bronze Age. Additionally, the Egyptian account of the battle, while detailed, is clearly propagandistic and may exaggerate Egyptian success. The Hittite account, if it survives, presents a different perspective. The 'inconclusive' outcome is a modern scholarly interpretation; both sides claimed victory in their own records. Furthermore, some scholars question whether the battle was as significant as traditional narratives suggest, or whether it was one of many conflicts in a prolonged struggle for Levantine control.
historiographic_note: The year 1300 BCE falls within a period of relatively rich documentation for the eastern Mediterranean and Near East, but the historical record is heavily skewed toward Egypt and the Hittite Empire. Mycenaean Greece is known primarily through archaeology, which provides limited chronological precision. Large regions of the world (Africa, Asia, the Americas, Northern Europe) have no contemporary written records for this period. The bias toward literate civilizations and toward military and political events means that economic, social, and cultural developments in less-documented regions are largely invisible to modern historians. The chronology of the period is uncertain by ±5–10 years or more, and the dating of individual events within a year is often impossible.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Kadesh and subsequent Egyptian-Hittite conflict led to the peace treaty of 1259 BCE
Mycenaean trade networks of this period were disrupted during the Late Bronze Age Collapse around 1200 BCE
Shang Dynasty bronze-working and oracle bone script traditions continued until the Shang-Zhou transition around 1046 BCE
